scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: The answer names every key file with a concrete role description (chat_completion.rb as 'core chat loop,' function_dispatch.rb as 'declarative function DSL,' function_tool_adapter.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, transcript_adapter.rb, mcp/, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb), giving the agent a complete file-level entry point; however, the organizing architecture — that these are Ruby modules a host class includes a la carte — is never stated, so the agent must infer the inclusion pattern before acting.

specificity.rationale: Paths are concrete throughout (lib/raix/chat_completion.rb, lib/raix/function_tool_adapter.rb, lib/raix/mcp/sse_client.rb, etc.) with line numbers supplied for most cited locations; no vague references like 'the chat code' appear.

justification.rationale: The answer names modules and their roles but never explains WHY the design uses composable mixins (so a host class adds chat, dispatch, and MCP behavior a la carte); the one near-miss is calling mcp.rb a 'concern' without drawing the architectural conclusion.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every detail with full confidence and flags no uncertainty about how the mixins compose onto a host class — the exact seam most likely to surprise an agent extending the pipeline (e.g., that FunctionDispatch.function writes into the host class's .functions, not the mixin's own).

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The 15-step ordered list in step_2_tool_call_round_trip gives file:line for every layer: entry at chat_completion.rb:73, model call at :147, tool_call extraction at :160, dispatch at :186 delegating to function_dispatch.rb:67 which appends the transcript, and recursion at :200 until a text response at :221. An agent could open each file at the cited line and change any layer with only targeted follow-up reads. The one gap is that max_tool_calls is not named as a check in the step ordering itself (it appears in step_6 but not in the step_2 trace steps).

specificity.rationale: Every step names the exact method and file:line rather than 'the chat loop' — dispatch_tool_function at :297, FunctionDispatch at function_dispatch.rb:67, and transcript append are all cited concretely. max_tool_calls is absent from the step_2 ordered trace (only surfacing in step_6), which is a gap for a step explicitly about the loop.

justification.rationale: Step 14 says the loop 'recursively calls chat_completion with the updated transcript until a text response is produced,' which explains continuation, but the answer does not explain WHY results re-enter (tool results appended to transcript become new context for the next model call) or WHY dispatch sends back to the same loop rather than returning. The steps describe WHAT at each point; the causal chain is thin.

uncertainty.rationale: The step_2 trace never explicitly flags where the agent should verify branching behavior — multiple tool calls in one response, the max_tool_calls cutoff path, and stop_tool_calls_and_respond are mentioned in step_6 as enumerated items but never marked 'verify before changing' in the round-trip trace, leaving the agent to infer which branches exist.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: step_3 maps every link in the chain with file:line: the function DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46, class-level storage at :27, tools accessor at :126-130, available_tools filtering in chat_completion.rb:91-97 ("nil=all, false=none, Array=filtered subset"), filtered_tools validation at :303-313, and schema conversion in function_tool_adapter.rb:6-48 and :50-57. An agent could add a new declared capability from this map with only targeted reads of those lines.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named exactly — FunctionDispatch.function at :46, functions attr_reader at :27, tools at :126-130, FunctionToolAdapter.create_tool_from_function at function_tool_adapter.rb:6-48, convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at :50-57, available_tools at chat_completion.rb:91-97 — with no vague references to "the function code".

justification.rationale: The answer describes WHAT each piece does ("DSL stores declaration in self.class.functions", "builds an anonymous RubyLLM::Tool subclass") but never explains WHY: why declarations live at the class level (shared across instances; composed once when the mixin is included), or why the adapter layer exists (provider abstraction, separation from Raix's own schema representation). The design rationale is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: No verification flags appear in step_3: the answer does not warn that the required/optional parameter schema format is a likely mistake point, that function names must be valid Ruby method identifiers, that nil vs false in available_tools have distinct semantics the agent should confirm, or that RubyLLM::Tool has its own schema expectations that the adapter must satisfy. The answer is uniformly confident with no "verify before acting" guidance.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three key adapter files appear with concrete file:line: base.rb lines 17-27/31-46, multimodal_content_adapter.rb lines 20-38, transcript_adapter.rb lines 14-80 and 82-119, plus the call sites in chat_completion.rb at lines 133, 138, and 358. The agent can see the full path from transcript storage through transformation to the request; the only minor gap is that the answer doesn't show how TranscriptAdapter output feeds into the MessageAdapters::Base chain in sequence.

specificity.rationale: Every adapter is named by exact file path and line range (e.g., 'lib/raix/message_adapters/base.rb lines 31-46', 'lib/raix/multimodal_content_adapter.rb lines 20-38') rather than 'the message code'; call sites in chat_completion.rb are also pinpointed (lines 133, 138, 358).

justification.rationale: The answer explains WHY multimodal_content_adapter exists ('so images reach the provider'), WHY base.rb applies cache_control ('long Claude-3 content to Anthropic cache_control multipart format when cache_at is set'), and WHY transcript_adapter flattens ('flattens them for requests'); it falls short of an overarching 'provider-shape normalization' framing that ties them together.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer mentions the cache_at condition and image_url arrays as facts but never flags them as places the agent should verify (e.g., whether cache_control applies only to Anthropic, how provider differences affect the tool-result shape); there are no explicit 'verify this' signals anywhere in the step_4 section.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers all required pieces with file:line: Tool value object at mcp/tool.rb:11-35, SSE client at sse_client.rb:32-77, stdio client at stdio_client.rb:16-52, and the critical registration path at mcp.rb:70-158 that includes FunctionDispatch and defines proxy methods. An agent could immediately open mcp.rb:70 and trace the full path.

specificity.rationale: Every MCP component is named by exact file and line range — mcp/tool.rb, mcp/sse_client.rb, mcp/stdio_client.rb, mcp.rb — with distinct roles (value object, tool list, tool call, registration/coercion). No vague 'the MCP code' references.

justification.rationale: The 'joins_same_dispatch' note explicitly states that MCP tools go through FunctionDispatch registration so they appear in self.class.functions and pass through FunctionToolAdapter exactly like local functions, which answers the WHY; however it does not explain the design motivation (one calling path for the model regardless of tool origin).

uncertainty.rationale: The answer makes no explicit flags about where the external path diverges from local functions — the schema source being remote, argument coercion differences, transport error modes — leaving the agent to assume parity without prompting verification of those seams.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer enumerates all six groups — chat loop (chat_completion.rb with 10+ line ranges), function declaration/schema (function_dispatch.rb, function_tool_adapter.rb), conversation adapters (base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb, transcript_adapter.rb), external tools (mcp.rb, mcp/tool.rb, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb), configuration, and a 12-file spec matrix — all with file:line. An agent could open PRs from this map alone.

specificity.rationale: Every site is cited with precise line numbers or ranges (e.g. 'lib/raix/chat_completion.rb:160-187', 'lib/raix/function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57', 'spec/raix/mcp/sse_spec.rb covers Live SSE MCP integration'), not a single vague phrase like 'the tool code'.

justification.rationale: The six categories implicitly signal runs/declares/converts/adapts/external/tests, and step_7 WHY lines ('Entry point, tool resolution... all live here', 'MCP tools must remain aligned...') are clear; but within step_6 itself the 'dependency' fields describe WHAT each range does rather than WHY that range is a contract site, leaving the agent to infer the causal link.

uncertainty.rationale: The only uncertainty flag is buried in step_7 notes ('VCR cassettes in spec/vcr/ will need regeneration'); step_6 itself never flags that mixins are only reached when included in a host class, that MCP transport paths differ from local dispatch in ways static reading can't confirm, or that dynamic dispatch may surface callers the enumeration missed.

map_quality.rationale: The edit map covers all four areas (chat loop, function DSL + schema, conversation adapters, external-tool path) plus specs and VCR cassettes, each with concrete file paths and arrays of specific line numbers. The coverage is complete enough for a teammate to open a PR, though the 'lines_to_touch' lists are exhaustive enumerations of every touched line rather than scoped change-point summaries, which slightly reduces actionability.

specificity.rationale: Every area names the exact files (lib/raix/chat_completion.rb, function_dispatch.rb, function_tool_adapter.rb, mcp/tool.rb, mcp/sse_client.rb, mcp/stdio_client.rb, transcript_adapter.rb) with explicit line arrays rather than vague descriptions, and all twelve spec files are named individually with their coverage purpose.

justification.rationale: Each section carries a 'why' field that explains the pipeline role (e.g., 'Entry point, tool resolution, tool-call extraction…' for chat_completion.rb; 'MCP tools must remain aligned with local function schema, dispatch, transcript feedback…' for the MCP area), but the explanations describe what the file owns rather than why a change to the contract forces a change there — slightly surface-level for the declared criterion.

uncertainty.rationale: Uncertainty flags are shallow: only 'VCR cassettes need regeneration' and 'do not test private methods' appear in the notes; there is no explicit acknowledgment of unknowns around how the loop re-enters after multiple tool calls, whether the schema format for required vs optional parameters covers edge cases, or whether MCP transport clients have additional path branches the static read may have missed.
